title: Netzl and Christina, two generations from the same winery in Austria’s Carnuntum region
summary: Website: https://www.netzl.com/en Many Austrian wineries are family owned, and the change of generations is a way that some use to
content:
Website: https://www.netzl.com/en

Many Austrian wineries are family owned, and the change of generations is a way that some use to reinvent themselves. That’s happening at Netzl, where Christina Netzl is making a new range of wines that exists alongside the traditional wines of her father Franz (they are pictured above). Both ranges are excellent, and it’s great to see this sort of reinvention.

The Netzl vineyard, near the village of Göttlesbrun, is 30 hectares, all farmed organically, with a split of 60:40 red to white. They focus on the hillside vineyard for their single-vineyard wines. These sites are surrounded by trees, which cools things down nicely. Soils are gravelly.

I tasted these wines with Christina at Vievinum in Vienna.

Netzl Chardonnay 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
This is a regional wine, and spends 5 months in stainless steel on full lees. Lively, bright fruit here with supple lemony notes and good acidity. 89/100

Netzel Göttlesbrunn 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
This is a blend of Chardonnay and Grüner Veltliner, with the Chardonnay spending a year in amphora (750 litre Tava) on full lees. Lovely textured and depth with pure citrus and pear fruit with some white peach richness. Fine grained. 92/100

Netzl Ried Altenberg Weissburgunder 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
Made in a mix of 500 litre tonneaux and amphorae. Complex and layered with fresh fruit, rounded texture and a slight salinity. Really good. 94/100

Netzl Ried Altenberg Chardonnay 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
Made in a mix of oak and amphorae. Lovely tension here and a bit of saltiness. Pure, fine and expressive. Delicate but with tension. 93/100

Netzl Rosanna Rosé 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
Zweigelt, half direct press, half saignée. Fresh, bright, pure, linear, quite transparent, easy. 88/100

Christina Grüner Veltliner 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
The only addition is 20 mg SO2 before bottling, and this wine is bottled with fine lees. Supple and textural with nice weight and a rounded palate. Notes of white pepper support the pear and peach fruit. 92/100

Christina Rosé 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
Zweigelt, mostly saignée. Juicy with nice weight. Fruity and expressive with red cherries and pear. Stylish and textural. 91/100

Christina Orange 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
This is Chardonnay, Sauvignon Blanc and Welschriesling, skin fermented separately and spending 3 weeks on skins. No added sulfites. Lovely weight of pear and peach fruit with notes of almond and mint. Good balance with some structure. 93/100

Christina Gelber Muskateller 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
Skin fermented, five weeks on skins, aged in 500 litre vats. Floral and grapey with nice weight and good structure. Has some pear and table grape fruit. Grippy finish. 92/100

Christina Kuhlbar 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
This is Zweigelt, with a portion direct pressed, the rest a short time on skins. A lighter-style red that’s fresh and juicy with nice texture to the cherry and plum fruit, backed up with good acidity. Stylish. 93/100

Christina St Laurent 2021 Carnuntum, Austria
11% alcohol. Whole bunch, semi-carbonic. Supple and juicy with a green hint, and nice freshness and weight on the palate. Evolving nicely, and still tart and fresh. 92/100

Christina Zweigelt 2023 Carnuntum, Austria
This is short maceration then ageing in big oak. Juicy and tart with lively sour cherry and plum fruit, with some grippy structure. Nice definition: lively and crunchy. 91/100

Netzl Rubin Carnuntum 2023 Austria
Rubin Carnuntum is a regional style of Zweigelt. This is fresh with nice texture. Bright with good concentration, this is juicy and stylish. 93/100

Netzl Prellenkirchen Blaufränkisch 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
This is made from grapes they buy in from the Spitzerberg. Concentrated, chalky and mineral with nice density. Stylish showing freshness and depth, as well as some structure. 94/100

Netzl Göttlesbrun Edes Tal 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
Zweigelt, Merlot and Syrah. Fresh, supple and spicy with nice structure. Quite classic in style. Has a chalky edge to the vivid fruit, with nice density and weight. Stylish. 93/100

Netzl St Laurent 2023 Carnuntum, Austria
Destemmed, short maceration, then goes to 500 litre barrels. Lively and rounded with supple fruit. There’s a sweet core to the palate, with nice texture and balance. 93/100

Netzl Zweigelt Ried Haidacker 1er 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
Loess and gravel soils, 10% whole cluster. Very fine, fresh and expressive with nice tannic structure and a floral edge to the fruit. Some well judged oak. 93/100

Netzl Zweigelt Ried Haidacker 1er 2020 Carnuntum, Austria
Supple and bright, and linear, with nice juiciness and some structure, finishing with a sour cherry twist. Very stylish. 94/100

Netzl Ried Bärnreiser 1er Anna-Christina 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
50% Zweigelt, 30% Blaufränkisch, 20% Merlot. Bright and elegant with nice concentration, good tannins, and nice focus and grip. Linear, with real energy, and a touch of oak. Still tightwound. 93/100

title: Filippi: natural expressions of Soave, Italy
summary: Filippi are based in Soave, and the property has 20 hectares of forest as well as 14 hectares of vines.
content:
Filippi are based in Soave, and the property has 20 hectares of forest as well as 14 hectares of vines. The highest point of the vineyard is 400 m, and this is a transition point between basaltic and limestone soils. Filippo Filippi has been making wines naturally here for more than 20 years now, and they are full of interest.

Winemaking is all in stainless steel tanks with wild yeast ferments.

Filippi Vigdagòti NV IGT Verona, Italy
11.5% alcohol. One litre bottle. Garganega. Textured and mineral with supple citrus, pear and green apple fruit, with nice spicy framing. Nutritious and delicious. 91/100

Filippi Castelcerino 2024 Soave Colli Scaligeri, Italy
11% alcohol. This is from volcanic soils. Supple, finely textured and quite mineral, with notes of lemons, green apple and a hint of melon. Nice stony mineral undercurrent to the sweet, lively fruit. 93/100

Filippi Monteseroni Garganega 2023 IGT Verona, Italy
Garganega from limestone. Sometimes this is bottled as Soave but it can fail the taste test at the commission. This is fine-grained, textural and grainy with nice acidity. So fine grained with a stony, mineral edge, showing good acidity but also some depth. Lovely. 94/100

Filippi Turbiani Trebbiano di Soave IGT Verona, Italy
This is from a massal selection of 30 year old vines in the forest, on limestone soils. Fine and expressive with a lovely mineral edge and real delicacy. Fine and stony with beautiful poise and elegance. 94/100

title: Three nice rosés: Boutari, Delaunay, Foncalieu
summary: It’s rosé season, and here are three attractive, well priced examples, including a nice one from Greece. Boutari Dianthos Xinomavro
content:
It’s rosé season, and here are three attractive, well priced examples, including a nice one from Greece.

Boutari Dianthos Xinomavro Rosé 2025 PGI Imathia, Naoussa, Greece
12.5% alcohol. This is fresh and lively with some mandarin and citrus notes, as well as red cherry and cranberry. It’s fruity and delicious, but there’s a brightness and verticality to it that makes it quite gastronomic. Very stylish and well priced, too. 90/100 (£10 Tesco)

Joël Delaunay Les Cabotines Rosé 2025 Touraine, Loire, France
12.5% alcohol. Thierry Delaunay is a well respected Touraine vigneron, and this wine is made from Pineau D’Aunis and Gamay. Pale in colour, it’s stony and bright with raspberry and cranberry fruit, as well as some juiciness. This has a delicious tartness with subtle sappy hints, and lots of gastronomic potential. 91/100

Foncalieu Le Versant Grenache Rosé 2025 IGP Pays d’Oc, France
13% alcohol. Beautifully presented with a painted label design from artist Barthélémy Lanzini. This is a deliciously expressive rose with a sweet core of cherries and berries, with a fine spicy edge. There’s texture and richness here, showing deliciousness and drinkability, but also some nice spicy detail and an innate fruit sweetness. 90/100

title: Videos: Robots in the vineyard, new technology applied
summary: Viticulture is expensive, and a lot of the cost is paying people to do quite boring, repetitive work. Of course,
content:
Viticulture is expensive, and a lot of the cost is paying people to do quite boring, repetitive work. Of course, it’s great to give people jobs, but in many regions worldwide it can be a challenge finding people, especially for seasonal tasks that require a lot of people. Here I’ve collated some YouTube videos of vineyard robotics, beginning with one of my own.

This is a film I shot of the Vitibot Bakus in operation in Burgundy – it’s now quite widely used in France

This is a robot called Frasky in operation in Italy

This is a robot called Burro Grande that can be used to mow

And here are some robots in action at Pernod Ricard’s Brancott Estate in New Zealand

Here’s a trial of a robot that can actually pick grapes, being used in the UK

And here’s another grape-picking robot

Another robot, called Traxx. This, like Bakus, is suited to low canopies and close-planted vineyards with its over-row design.

Vinbot is used to estimate yield – an important job

These are Thorvald robots used to deliver UV-C to defeat powdery mildew. They need to be used in the dark.

Same technology: UV-C robots

And here’s a trial of a legged robot! Can do rocky vineyards.

title: Highlights: two from low intervention English producer Matt Gregory
summary: Website: https://mattgregorywines.co.uk/ Matt Gregory makes wines from a single 3 hectare vineyard in Leicestershire (UK), a county not normally associated
content:
Website: https://mattgregorywines.co.uk/

Matt Gregory makes wines from a single 3 hectare vineyard in Leicestershire (UK), a county not normally associated with winegrowing. The wines are naturally made, in small quantities, and they are fabulous.

Matt Gregory Wines Little Sister NV England
9% alcohol. This is a blend of two vintages.From 2023, 20% Pinot Noir, 14% Pinot Gris and from 2024, 47% Bacchus, 10% Regent, 9% Seyval Blanc. Direct pressed Bacchus from 2024 was aged in stainless steel and then blended with co-fermented Regent and Seyval after the Winter. A single barrel of Pinot Noir and Pinot Gris from the 2023 vintage was also then blended in. The only addition was a small sulfur dioxide addition before bottling in August 2025. This is pale in colour, and light and expressive on the nose with green-tinged red cherries, cranberries and elderflower. The palate is fresh and juicy with good acidity and some lovely lemon, mandarin and cherry notes, with a touch of redcurrant. It’s like a clarete in style, with more colour and structure than a rose, but it behaves quite like a white wine gastronomically, with keen acidity. The low alcohol really brings out the tart qualities of the fruit really nicely. There’s enough complexity to hold the interest, and it’s a beautifully expressive, personality-full wine. 93/100 (£27.50)

Matt Gregory Wines Two Sisters 2023 England
10% alcohol. 60/40 Pinot Noir/Pinot Gris. Destemmed and co-fermented, then pressed to stainless steel and a single used barrique from Barolo (25% of the blend). Only addition was minor sulfite add before bottling. Quite pale in colour, this has a sappy nose with green leafy notes and some cherry and redcurrant. The palate shows some structure and a hint of savoury spiciness, as well as tart red fruits, but also a bit of ripe pear richness, and some wild strawberry. There’s a lot going on here: a lighter-style red with real personality and character. 93/100 (£37.50)

title: Highlights: Champagne Gosset Cuvée Dosage Zéro
summary: Website: https://www.champagne-gosset.com/en/our-champagne-wines/cuvee-zero-dosage/ This is a new cuvée from Gosset. The wine is based on the 2013 vintage and was bottled
content:
Website: https://www.champagne-gosset.com/en/our-champagne-wines/cuvee-zero-dosage/

This is a new cuvée from Gosset. The wine is based on the 2013 vintage and was bottled in 2015, then disgorged 10 years later. The base was given more than a year on lees to add richness and complexity. This is quite beautiful.

Champagne Gosset Cuvée Dosage Zéro NV France
12% alcohol. Chardonnay 62%, Pinot Noir 38%, from a range of villages including Ambonnay, Avize, Bouzy, Cumières, Cramant, Villers-Marmery and Rilly la Montagne. Taut and expressive with lovely acidity supporting the rich, crystalline citrus fruit, together with a hint of apple and a long, tangy, slightly saline finish. This is vinous and delicious, and so fresh and gastronomic. A brilliant wine. 95/100

title: Brent Marris, Emma Marris and The Ned, tasting wines from one of Marlborough’s (NZ) emerging dynasties
summary: The Marris dynasty are an important part of Marlborough’s past, present, and surely future. John Marris was the land agent
content:
The Marris dynasty are an important part of Marlborough’s past, present, and surely future. John Marris was the land agent that Frank Yukich of (then) Montana used when he swooped in and bought some 500 hectares of lousy farmland in Marlborough, which was then not a wine region, back in 1973. It’s a remarkable story, and one I’d love to write up in more detail. The genius was that he did a lot all at once, rather than trialling a bit of land with a vineyard, then coming back for more: if farmers got hint that New Zealand’s biggest wine country thought their land was special, the price would have gone up. It turns out that John didn’t make much money despite working on a very big deal.

Brent, his son (pictured above), made his fame through working at Delgats, starting back in 1985 and launching Oyster Bay, one of Marlborough’s most successful wine brands. Then he established Wither Hills with his father, which they then sold to Lion Nathan. I first met him when he was the Wither Hills boss, along with his winemaker Ben Glover, back in 2006 (here). Fast forward 20 years and he now has an amazing property in the Waihopai Valley called Leefield Station (650 hectares of vines). From here he makes a wide range of wines, and also has a few other smaller vineyards. The Ned is the more commercial end, but here we tried the fancy end of the range, and they are excellent. The overarching brand is Marisco Vineyards.

Emma is Brent’s daughter, and now an established winemaker. She’s doing some wines under her name here, and, again, they are looking to play at a higher level. They succeed.

I tasted these wines with Brent at Wine Paris in February 2026.

Emma Marris

Emma Marris Sauvignon Blanc 2025 Marlborough, New Zealand
This is made with juice stabulation before fermentation. Lovely aromatics, with some floral character and a touch of passionfruit, leading to a palate that’s juicy, complex and nicely textured. Stylish. 93/100

Emma Marris Chardonnay 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
Hand picked and whole bunch pressed. Lovely mealy edge to the focused citrus and white peach fruit. Good balance with nice density. 93/100

Emma Marris Pinot Noir 2023 Marlborough, New Zealand
Lovely aromatics here, with a nice spicy, slightly peppery edge to the floral cherry fruit. Nice texture on the palate: silky and fine, showing good balance, appropriate structure and nice open fruit. 93/100

The Ned

The Ned 909 Sauvignon Blanc 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
This is from small parcels at Leefield Station that are a bit stonier. Hand picked before the main harvest, whole bunch pressed into a large wooden oval and amphorae, kept on yeast lees for 3 months. Very fine and expressive with lovely concentration. Stony and mineral with lovely balance and focus, with some brightness and a hint of grapefruit. 94/100

The Ned Skyscraper Sauvignon Blanc 2025 Marlborough, New Zealand
Lovely aromatics here: pure and fine with some passionfruit and just a hint of boxwood. Green notes on the palate with nice texture. Dry and finely structured with good acidity and great balance. 92/100

The Ned Skyscraper Chardonnay 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
Hand picked, warm ferment in French oak puncheons, 40% malolactic. Very stylish with lovely balance. Citrus, pear and a hint of peach. Nice mealy edge and also some structure. Subtle toastiness. 93/100

The Ned Skyscraper Pinot Noir 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
20% whole bunch, matured in French oak puncheons, 50% new. Lovely ripe plum and cherry fruit with some berries and a bit of richness. Stylish and poised with nice texture. Very expressive. 93/100

The Ned Skyscraper Syrah 2023 Marlborough, New Zealand
Brent has planted 3 hectares of Syrah at Leefield, on a hotter spot on one of the terraces. Supple and fresh with nice brightness, showing a touch of pepper as well as sleek fruit. Expressive cool climate Syrah. 93/100

title: Brunia, an exciting cool-climate wine project from South Africa’s Walker Bay
summary: Website: https://www.bruniawines.co.za/ This is an exciting project owned by the Sander family, and it’s in a relatively unknown winegrowing region
content:
Website: https://www.bruniawines.co.za/

This is an exciting project owned by the Sander family, and it’s in a relatively unknown winegrowing region that’s called Sondagskloof. Let’s locate this geographically. It’s in the Walker Bay region, but it’s best to think of it as extension of the Cape South Coast. If you head to Hermanus on the R43, there’s a road that leads up the Hemel-en-Arde Valley, which is now a very exciting wine region with a distinctly cool climate. But you don’t take that: you keep going through the town and stay on the R43 to a town called Stanford. Then you keep going, and you find Sondagskloof about half way between Hermanus and Elim, some 17 km from the cold sea.

There’s a bit of altitude here, but not too much, and the climate is ideal for cooler-climate varieties. The farm is called Cold Mountain, and it’s quite big, but there are a modest 17 hectares of vines, from which Wade Sander (pictured above) makes some very smart wines. He trained at Elsenberg and worked a few harvests around and about, and then did six years at Mullineux before heading up this family-owned operation, alongside his brother Brett who oversees the rest of the farm. I tasted these wines with him at ProWein in March 2026.

Farming here, across the 400 hectare property, is organic and biodynamic.

Brunia Sauvignon Blanc 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
25 year old vines, all barrel fermented, with the only addition some SO2 at bottling. Textural with lovely purity. There’s a mineral core to the juicy berry fruits with some citrus brightness. Really intriguing, showing lovely weight. 94/100

Brunia White 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
50% Sauvignon, 50% Semillon, raised in barrel. Blended after 6 months and then returned to barrel for 4 more months. Fine spicy detail here: textured and lively with an appealing stony mineral edge to the ripe pear and citrus fruit. Very textural with nice concentration. 94/100

Brunia Extended Barrel Ageing Sauvignon Blanc 2019 Sondagskloof, South Africa
Lovely depth here with apples, spice and even a hint of honey. Nice depth and focus with some richness, countered by a good acid line. This is really good. 94/100

Brunia Pinot Noir 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
This is from the youngest block on the farm: 115 clone planted on sandstone 16 years ago. 10% whole cluster. Supple and elegant. Refined with some mid-palate weight, fine spicy notes and a touch of iodine. Detailed, mineral and very fine. 95/100

Brunia Syrah 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
40% whole bunch, then 12 months in older oak. This is lively and balanced with nice purity and some white pepper. Some fine spicy detail here. Sleek with fresh red cherry and raspberry fruit, showing real finesse. 94/100

title: Cantina Antonioli are making lovely natural wines in the foothills of the Apennine mountain range in Italy’s Umbria
summary: Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/cantinaantonioli/?hl=en Giulia and Giorgio Antonioli came to wine as a second career: she was an archaeologist and he was
content:
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/cantinaantonioli/?hl=en

Giulia and Giorgio Antonioli came to wine as a second career: she was an archaeologist and he was an art historian. But in 2018 they changed direction and rented some old vineyards on the slopes of the Apennine Mountains in Umbria (just to the south than Tuscany in the middle of the country) and renovating them.

Now Cantino Antonioli works with a selection of 60-120 year old vine plots on two different soil types: limestone and clay. The two main white varieties are Trebbiano Toscana and Malvasia.

Viticulture is enlightened, and winemaking is natural. This is a fabulous project. I tasted these wines at VinNatur in Paris, November 2025.

Cantina Antonioli Antimes Branco 2022 Umbria, Italy
This is direct pressed to cement tank, where it ferments and then ages for two years. Full yellow colour. Nice expressive pear fruit with lovely balance, showing supple citrus fruit and orange peel, with a hint of mint. Midweight, fresh and expressive, this is delicious. 92/100

Cantina Antonioli Ambratile Branco 2022 Umbria, Italy
12% alcohol. Two years in cement. Lively and bright with a stony edge to the fruit, as well as a bit of structure. Notes of apple, mandarin, orange peel and mint with an appealing wet clay/stone edge. Distinctive. 93/100

Cantina Antonioli Il Male Minore Rosato 2023 Umbria, Italy
Trebbiano Toscana and Sangiovese, one year in fibre glass. Pale pink/orange colour. Complex nose of mint, cherry and apple. The palate is textural, fine grained and well balanced, showing good restraint. 92/100

Cantina Antonioli Il Male Minore Rosso 2021 Umbria, Italy
Merlot and Cielegiolo. Vibrant and juicy with cherries, herbs and spice. Has some sour cherry and a slight bitter tang. Good energy here. 90/100

Cantina Antonioli Grabovio Rosso 2021 Umbria, Italy
13% alcohol. Merlot, Cielegiolo, Sangiovese and Barbera from limestone soils. Luicy and lively with nice acidity. Tart redcurrant and cherry fruit with a slight savoury twist. Supple and linear with a sour cherry finish. 92/100

Cantina Antonioli Agreste Elegante 2021 Umbria, Italy
13.5% alcohol. Merlot, Sangiovese, Cielegiolo, Barbera from clay soils. Juicy, bright and lively with nice tart cherry and plum fruit as well as some structure. Vivid raspberry notes, too. Nice balance with the juicy fruit to the fore. 93/100

title: La Biancara di Angiolino e Alessandro Maule: meet one of Italy’s most celebrated natural wine producers
summary: Website: https://www.angiolinomaule.com/en-home Angiolino Maule is a leading figure in the Italian natural wine scene. He and his wife Rosamaria began
content:
Website: https://www.angiolinomaule.com/en-home

Angiolino Maule is a leading figure in the Italian natural wine scene. He and his wife Rosamaria began a pizzeria in the late 1970s, and saved enough money to buy a farm, La Biancara, with some vineyards. They kept running their hospitality operation, and made their first wines, conventionally, in 1988. But they became friends with winegrowers who were trying to make wines naturally, and this appealed to them. It wasn’t an easy journey, but they persevered and Angiolino founded the VinNatur wine association in 2006. It’s one of the most forward-thinking of all the natural wine associations, and has a research element where new ways of working in the vineyard and winery are being trialled and measured.

They have 18 hectares in 13 plots around the town of Gambellara, which is in the Veneto, just northeast of Soave. Garganega is the main grape variety here, but they also have some Merlot and some Grenache (known here as Tai rosso).

Angiolino is helped out by two of his four sons who work with him in the vineyard and cellar, Alessandro and Tommaso, pictured above.

Angiolino Maule Garg’n’Go Garganega Frizzante 2024 Veneto, Italy
A sparkling wine refermented in bottle with must. Lively, bright, appley and delicious, with fresh citrus fruit and a hint of grape. Rich and expressive. 91/100

Angiolino Maule Masieri 2024 Veneto, Italy
11% alcohol. This is the entry level wine, direct pressed to stainless steel. No added sulfites. Lovely freshness here with apple and pear fruit, showing some grapey depth. Nice freshness with a bit of salinity. Good purity and massive drinkability. 93/100

Angiolino Maule Sassaia 2024 Veneto, Italy
11.5% alcohol. One day skin fermentation then fermented in 3000 litre oak. No added sulfites. Golden in colour, this is rich, smooth and textured with nice depth of ripe pear fruit and some peachy richness. Has a touch of melon, too. Harmonious and broad with nice depth. 94/100

Angiolino Taibane 2021 Maule Veneto, Italy
13.5% alcohol. This is not made every year. Harvested at the end of November, this shows nuts, honey and spice with some spicy oak. A very rich style with a savoury edge. 91/100

Angiolino Maule Masieri Rosso 2024 Veneto, Italy
50% Merlot, 50% Tai rosso (Grenache), from the limestone hills of the Colli Berichi. Lively and fresh with supple red cherry and redcurrant fruit. Juicy and grainy with nice freshness and a hint of green. Shows good acidity. 93/100

Angiolino Maule So San 2021 Veneto, Italy
16.5% alcohol. This is Tai rosso (Grenache), macerated for a month then aged two years in big barrels. Bold and brooding with a savoury, spicy edge to the sweet berry fruits. There’s some silkiness to the fruit, but also some spice and earth, and some grip. An interesting and unique wine. 93/100

Angiolino Maule Monte Sorio 2018 Veneto, Italy
Garganega, dried for 2 months, then given some skin maceration. 70 g/l sugar. Rich and bold with notes of tea, spice, earth and some honey. Real intensity here with grippy structure and a spicy frill. 92/100

title: Netzl and Christina, two generations from the same winery in Austria’s Carnuntum region
summary: Website: https://www.netzl.com/en Many Austrian wineries are family owned, and the change of generations is a way that some use to
content:
Website: https://www.netzl.com/en

Many Austrian wineries are family owned, and the change of generations is a way that some use to reinvent themselves. That’s happening at Netzl, where Christina Netzl is making a new range of wines that exists alongside the traditional wines of her father Franz (they are pictured above). Both ranges are excellent, and it’s great to see this sort of reinvention.

The Netzl vineyard, near the village of Göttlesbrun, is 30 hectares, all farmed organically, with a split of 60:40 red to white. They focus on the hillside vineyard for their single-vineyard wines. These sites are surrounded by trees, which cools things down nicely. Soils are gravelly.

I tasted these wines with Christina at Vievinum in Vienna.

Netzl Chardonnay 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
This is a regional wine, and spends 5 months in stainless steel on full lees. Lively, bright fruit here with supple lemony notes and good acidity. 89/100

Netzel Göttlesbrunn 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
This is a blend of Chardonnay and Grüner Veltliner, with the Chardonnay spending a year in amphora (750 litre Tava) on full lees. Lovely textured and depth with pure citrus and pear fruit with some white peach richness. Fine grained. 92/100

Netzl Ried Altenberg Weissburgunder 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
Made in a mix of 500 litre tonneaux and amphorae. Complex and layered with fresh fruit, rounded texture and a slight salinity. Really good. 94/100

Netzl Ried Altenberg Chardonnay 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
Made in a mix of oak and amphorae. Lovely tension here and a bit of saltiness. Pure, fine and expressive. Delicate but with tension. 93/100

Netzl Rosanna Rosé 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
Zweigelt, half direct press, half saignée. Fresh, bright, pure, linear, quite transparent, easy. 88/100

Christina Grüner Veltliner 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
The only addition is 20 mg SO2 before bottling, and this wine is bottled with fine lees. Supple and textural with nice weight and a rounded palate. Notes of white pepper support the pear and peach fruit. 92/100

Christina Rosé 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
Zweigelt, mostly saignée. Juicy with nice weight. Fruity and expressive with red cherries and pear. Stylish and textural. 91/100

Christina Orange 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
This is Chardonnay, Sauvignon Blanc and Welschriesling, skin fermented separately and spending 3 weeks on skins. No added sulfites. Lovely weight of pear and peach fruit with notes of almond and mint. Good balance with some structure. 93/100

Christina Gelber Muskateller 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
Skin fermented, five weeks on skins, aged in 500 litre vats. Floral and grapey with nice weight and good structure. Has some pear and table grape fruit. Grippy finish. 92/100

Christina Kuhlbar 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
This is Zweigelt, with a portion direct pressed, the rest a short time on skins. A lighter-style red that’s fresh and juicy with nice texture to the cherry and plum fruit, backed up with good acidity. Stylish. 93/100

Christina St Laurent 2021 Carnuntum, Austria
11% alcohol. Whole bunch, semi-carbonic. Supple and juicy with a green hint, and nice freshness and weight on the palate. Evolving nicely, and still tart and fresh. 92/100

Christina Zweigelt 2023 Carnuntum, Austria
This is short maceration then ageing in big oak. Juicy and tart with lively sour cherry and plum fruit, with some grippy structure. Nice definition: lively and crunchy. 91/100

Netzl Rubin Carnuntum 2023 Austria
Rubin Carnuntum is a regional style of Zweigelt. This is fresh with nice texture. Bright with good concentration, this is juicy and stylish. 93/100

Netzl Prellenkirchen Blaufränkisch 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
This is made from grapes they buy in from the Spitzerberg. Concentrated, chalky and mineral with nice density. Stylish showing freshness and depth, as well as some structure. 94/100

Netzl Göttlesbrun Edes Tal 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
Zweigelt, Merlot and Syrah. Fresh, supple and spicy with nice structure. Quite classic in style. Has a chalky edge to the vivid fruit, with nice density and weight. Stylish. 93/100

Netzl St Laurent 2023 Carnuntum, Austria
Destemmed, short maceration, then goes to 500 litre barrels. Lively and rounded with supple fruit. There’s a sweet core to the palate, with nice texture and balance. 93/100

Netzl Zweigelt Ried Haidacker 1er 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
Loess and gravel soils, 10% whole cluster. Very fine, fresh and expressive with nice tannic structure and a floral edge to the fruit. Some well judged oak. 93/100

Netzl Zweigelt Ried Haidacker 1er 2020 Carnuntum, Austria
Supple and bright, and linear, with nice juiciness and some structure, finishing with a sour cherry twist. Very stylish. 94/100

Netzl Ried Bärnreiser 1er Anna-Christina 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
50% Zweigelt, 30% Blaufränkisch, 20% Merlot. Bright and elegant with nice concentration, good tannins, and nice focus and grip. Linear, with real energy, and a touch of oak. Still tightwound. 93/100

title: Filippi: natural expressions of Soave, Italy
summary: Filippi are based in Soave, and the property has 20 hectares of forest as well as 14 hectares of vines.
content:
Filippi are based in Soave, and the property has 20 hectares of forest as well as 14 hectares of vines. The highest point of the vineyard is 400 m, and this is a transition point between basaltic and limestone soils. Filippo Filippi has been making wines naturally here for more than 20 years now, and they are full of interest.

Winemaking is all in stainless steel tanks with wild yeast ferments.

Filippi Vigdagòti NV IGT Verona, Italy
11.5% alcohol. One litre bottle. Garganega. Textured and mineral with supple citrus, pear and green apple fruit, with nice spicy framing. Nutritious and delicious. 91/100

Filippi Castelcerino 2024 Soave Colli Scaligeri, Italy
11% alcohol. This is from volcanic soils. Supple, finely textured and quite mineral, with notes of lemons, green apple and a hint of melon. Nice stony mineral undercurrent to the sweet, lively fruit. 93/100

Filippi Monteseroni Garganega 2023 IGT Verona, Italy
Garganega from limestone. Sometimes this is bottled as Soave but it can fail the taste test at the commission. This is fine-grained, textural and grainy with nice acidity. So fine grained with a stony, mineral edge, showing good acidity but also some depth. Lovely. 94/100

Filippi Turbiani Trebbiano di Soave IGT Verona, Italy
This is from a massal selection of 30 year old vines in the forest, on limestone soils. Fine and expressive with a lovely mineral edge and real delicacy. Fine and stony with beautiful poise and elegance. 94/100

title: Three nice rosés: Boutari, Delaunay, Foncalieu
summary: It’s rosé season, and here are three attractive, well priced examples, including a nice one from Greece. Boutari Dianthos Xinomavro
content:
It’s rosé season, and here are three attractive, well priced examples, including a nice one from Greece.

Boutari Dianthos Xinomavro Rosé 2025 PGI Imathia, Naoussa, Greece
12.5% alcohol. This is fresh and lively with some mandarin and citrus notes, as well as red cherry and cranberry. It’s fruity and delicious, but there’s a brightness and verticality to it that makes it quite gastronomic. Very stylish and well priced, too. 90/100 (£10 Tesco)

Joël Delaunay Les Cabotines Rosé 2025 Touraine, Loire, France
12.5% alcohol. Thierry Delaunay is a well respected Touraine vigneron, and this wine is made from Pineau D’Aunis and Gamay. Pale in colour, it’s stony and bright with raspberry and cranberry fruit, as well as some juiciness. This has a delicious tartness with subtle sappy hints, and lots of gastronomic potential. 91/100

Foncalieu Le Versant Grenache Rosé 2025 IGP Pays d’Oc, France
13% alcohol. Beautifully presented with a painted label design from artist Barthélémy Lanzini. This is a deliciously expressive rose with a sweet core of cherries and berries, with a fine spicy edge. There’s texture and richness here, showing deliciousness and drinkability, but also some nice spicy detail and an innate fruit sweetness. 90/100

title: Videos: Robots in the vineyard, new technology applied
summary: Viticulture is expensive, and a lot of the cost is paying people to do quite boring, repetitive work. Of course,
content:
Viticulture is expensive, and a lot of the cost is paying people to do quite boring, repetitive work. Of course, it’s great to give people jobs, but in many regions worldwide it can be a challenge finding people, especially for seasonal tasks that require a lot of people. Here I’ve collated some YouTube videos of vineyard robotics, beginning with one of my own.

This is a film I shot of the Vitibot Bakus in operation in Burgundy – it’s now quite widely used in France

This is a robot called Frasky in operation in Italy

This is a robot called Burro Grande that can be used to mow

And here are some robots in action at Pernod Ricard’s Brancott Estate in New Zealand

Here’s a trial of a robot that can actually pick grapes, being used in the UK

And here’s another grape-picking robot

Another robot, called Traxx. This, like Bakus, is suited to low canopies and close-planted vineyards with its over-row design.

Vinbot is used to estimate yield – an important job

These are Thorvald robots used to deliver UV-C to defeat powdery mildew. They need to be used in the dark.

Same technology: UV-C robots

And here’s a trial of a legged robot! Can do rocky vineyards.

title: Highlights: two from low intervention English producer Matt Gregory
summary: Website: https://mattgregorywines.co.uk/ Matt Gregory makes wines from a single 3 hectare vineyard in Leicestershire (UK), a county not normally associated
content:
Website: https://mattgregorywines.co.uk/

Matt Gregory makes wines from a single 3 hectare vineyard in Leicestershire (UK), a county not normally associated with winegrowing. The wines are naturally made, in small quantities, and they are fabulous.

Matt Gregory Wines Little Sister NV England
9% alcohol. This is a blend of two vintages.From 2023, 20% Pinot Noir, 14% Pinot Gris and from 2024, 47% Bacchus, 10% Regent, 9% Seyval Blanc. Direct pressed Bacchus from 2024 was aged in stainless steel and then blended with co-fermented Regent and Seyval after the Winter. A single barrel of Pinot Noir and Pinot Gris from the 2023 vintage was also then blended in. The only addition was a small sulfur dioxide addition before bottling in August 2025. This is pale in colour, and light and expressive on the nose with green-tinged red cherries, cranberries and elderflower. The palate is fresh and juicy with good acidity and some lovely lemon, mandarin and cherry notes, with a touch of redcurrant. It’s like a clarete in style, with more colour and structure than a rose, but it behaves quite like a white wine gastronomically, with keen acidity. The low alcohol really brings out the tart qualities of the fruit really nicely. There’s enough complexity to hold the interest, and it’s a beautifully expressive, personality-full wine. 93/100 (£27.50)

Matt Gregory Wines Two Sisters 2023 England
10% alcohol. 60/40 Pinot Noir/Pinot Gris. Destemmed and co-fermented, then pressed to stainless steel and a single used barrique from Barolo (25% of the blend). Only addition was minor sulfite add before bottling. Quite pale in colour, this has a sappy nose with green leafy notes and some cherry and redcurrant. The palate shows some structure and a hint of savoury spiciness, as well as tart red fruits, but also a bit of ripe pear richness, and some wild strawberry. There’s a lot going on here: a lighter-style red with real personality and character. 93/100 (£37.50)

title: Highlights: Champagne Gosset Cuvée Dosage Zéro
summary: Website: https://www.champagne-gosset.com/en/our-champagne-wines/cuvee-zero-dosage/ This is a new cuvée from Gosset. The wine is based on the 2013 vintage and was bottled
content:
Website: https://www.champagne-gosset.com/en/our-champagne-wines/cuvee-zero-dosage/

This is a new cuvée from Gosset. The wine is based on the 2013 vintage and was bottled in 2015, then disgorged 10 years later. The base was given more than a year on lees to add richness and complexity. This is quite beautiful.

Champagne Gosset Cuvée Dosage Zéro NV France
12% alcohol. Chardonnay 62%, Pinot Noir 38%, from a range of villages including Ambonnay, Avize, Bouzy, Cumières, Cramant, Villers-Marmery and Rilly la Montagne. Taut and expressive with lovely acidity supporting the rich, crystalline citrus fruit, together with a hint of apple and a long, tangy, slightly saline finish. This is vinous and delicious, and so fresh and gastronomic. A brilliant wine. 95/100

title: Brent Marris, Emma Marris and The Ned, tasting wines from one of Marlborough’s (NZ) emerging dynasties
summary: The Marris dynasty are an important part of Marlborough’s past, present, and surely future. John Marris was the land agent
content:
The Marris dynasty are an important part of Marlborough’s past, present, and surely future. John Marris was the land agent that Frank Yukich of (then) Montana used when he swooped in and bought some 500 hectares of lousy farmland in Marlborough, which was then not a wine region, back in 1973. It’s a remarkable story, and one I’d love to write up in more detail. The genius was that he did a lot all at once, rather than trialling a bit of land with a vineyard, then coming back for more: if farmers got hint that New Zealand’s biggest wine country thought their land was special, the price would have gone up. It turns out that John didn’t make much money despite working on a very big deal.

Brent, his son (pictured above), made his fame through working at Delgats, starting back in 1985 and launching Oyster Bay, one of Marlborough’s most successful wine brands. Then he established Wither Hills with his father, which they then sold to Lion Nathan. I first met him when he was the Wither Hills boss, along with his winemaker Ben Glover, back in 2006 (here). Fast forward 20 years and he now has an amazing property in the Waihopai Valley called Leefield Station (650 hectares of vines). From here he makes a wide range of wines, and also has a few other smaller vineyards. The Ned is the more commercial end, but here we tried the fancy end of the range, and they are excellent. The overarching brand is Marisco Vineyards.

Emma is Brent’s daughter, and now an established winemaker. She’s doing some wines under her name here, and, again, they are looking to play at a higher level. They succeed.

I tasted these wines with Brent at Wine Paris in February 2026.

Emma Marris

Emma Marris Sauvignon Blanc 2025 Marlborough, New Zealand
This is made with juice stabulation before fermentation. Lovely aromatics, with some floral character and a touch of passionfruit, leading to a palate that’s juicy, complex and nicely textured. Stylish. 93/100

Emma Marris Chardonnay 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
Hand picked and whole bunch pressed. Lovely mealy edge to the focused citrus and white peach fruit. Good balance with nice density. 93/100

Emma Marris Pinot Noir 2023 Marlborough, New Zealand
Lovely aromatics here, with a nice spicy, slightly peppery edge to the floral cherry fruit. Nice texture on the palate: silky and fine, showing good balance, appropriate structure and nice open fruit. 93/100

The Ned

The Ned 909 Sauvignon Blanc 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
This is from small parcels at Leefield Station that are a bit stonier. Hand picked before the main harvest, whole bunch pressed into a large wooden oval and amphorae, kept on yeast lees for 3 months. Very fine and expressive with lovely concentration. Stony and mineral with lovely balance and focus, with some brightness and a hint of grapefruit. 94/100

The Ned Skyscraper Sauvignon Blanc 2025 Marlborough, New Zealand
Lovely aromatics here: pure and fine with some passionfruit and just a hint of boxwood. Green notes on the palate with nice texture. Dry and finely structured with good acidity and great balance. 92/100

The Ned Skyscraper Chardonnay 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
Hand picked, warm ferment in French oak puncheons, 40% malolactic. Very stylish with lovely balance. Citrus, pear and a hint of peach. Nice mealy edge and also some structure. Subtle toastiness. 93/100

The Ned Skyscraper Pinot Noir 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
20% whole bunch, matured in French oak puncheons, 50% new. Lovely ripe plum and cherry fruit with some berries and a bit of richness. Stylish and poised with nice texture. Very expressive. 93/100

The Ned Skyscraper Syrah 2023 Marlborough, New Zealand
Brent has planted 3 hectares of Syrah at Leefield, on a hotter spot on one of the terraces. Supple and fresh with nice brightness, showing a touch of pepper as well as sleek fruit. Expressive cool climate Syrah. 93/100

title: Brunia, an exciting cool-climate wine project from South Africa’s Walker Bay
summary: Website: https://www.bruniawines.co.za/ This is an exciting project owned by the Sander family, and it’s in a relatively unknown winegrowing region
content:
Website: https://www.bruniawines.co.za/

This is an exciting project owned by the Sander family, and it’s in a relatively unknown winegrowing region that’s called Sondagskloof. Let’s locate this geographically. It’s in the Walker Bay region, but it’s best to think of it as extension of the Cape South Coast. If you head to Hermanus on the R43, there’s a road that leads up the Hemel-en-Arde Valley, which is now a very exciting wine region with a distinctly cool climate. But you don’t take that: you keep going through the town and stay on the R43 to a town called Stanford. Then you keep going, and you find Sondagskloof about half way between Hermanus and Elim, some 17 km from the cold sea.

There’s a bit of altitude here, but not too much, and the climate is ideal for cooler-climate varieties. The farm is called Cold Mountain, and it’s quite big, but there are a modest 17 hectares of vines, from which Wade Sander (pictured above) makes some very smart wines. He trained at Elsenberg and worked a few harvests around and about, and then did six years at Mullineux before heading up this family-owned operation, alongside his brother Brett who oversees the rest of the farm. I tasted these wines with him at ProWein in March 2026.

Farming here, across the 400 hectare property, is organic and biodynamic.

Brunia Sauvignon Blanc 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
25 year old vines, all barrel fermented, with the only addition some SO2 at bottling. Textural with lovely purity. There’s a mineral core to the juicy berry fruits with some citrus brightness. Really intriguing, showing lovely weight. 94/100

Brunia White 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
50% Sauvignon, 50% Semillon, raised in barrel. Blended after 6 months and then returned to barrel for 4 more months. Fine spicy detail here: textured and lively with an appealing stony mineral edge to the ripe pear and citrus fruit. Very textural with nice concentration. 94/100

Brunia Extended Barrel Ageing Sauvignon Blanc 2019 Sondagskloof, South Africa
Lovely depth here with apples, spice and even a hint of honey. Nice depth and focus with some richness, countered by a good acid line. This is really good. 94/100

Brunia Pinot Noir 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
This is from the youngest block on the farm: 115 clone planted on sandstone 16 years ago. 10% whole cluster. Supple and elegant. Refined with some mid-palate weight, fine spicy notes and a touch of iodine. Detailed, mineral and very fine. 95/100

Brunia Syrah 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
40% whole bunch, then 12 months in older oak. This is lively and balanced with nice purity and some white pepper. Some fine spicy detail here. Sleek with fresh red cherry and raspberry fruit, showing real finesse. 94/100

title: Cantina Antonioli are making lovely natural wines in the foothills of the Apennine mountain range in Italy’s Umbria
summary: Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/cantinaantonioli/?hl=en Giulia and Giorgio Antonioli came to wine as a second career: she was an archaeologist and he was
content:
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/cantinaantonioli/?hl=en

Giulia and Giorgio Antonioli came to wine as a second career: she was an archaeologist and he was an art historian. But in 2018 they changed direction and rented some old vineyards on the slopes of the Apennine Mountains in Umbria (just to the south than Tuscany in the middle of the country) and renovating them.

Now Cantino Antonioli works with a selection of 60-120 year old vine plots on two different soil types: limestone and clay. The two main white varieties are Trebbiano Toscana and Malvasia.

Viticulture is enlightened, and winemaking is natural. This is a fabulous project. I tasted these wines at VinNatur in Paris, November 2025.

Cantina Antonioli Antimes Branco 2022 Umbria, Italy
This is direct pressed to cement tank, where it ferments and then ages for two years. Full yellow colour. Nice expressive pear fruit with lovely balance, showing supple citrus fruit and orange peel, with a hint of mint. Midweight, fresh and expressive, this is delicious. 92/100

Cantina Antonioli Ambratile Branco 2022 Umbria, Italy
12% alcohol. Two years in cement. Lively and bright with a stony edge to the fruit, as well as a bit of structure. Notes of apple, mandarin, orange peel and mint with an appealing wet clay/stone edge. Distinctive. 93/100

Cantina Antonioli Il Male Minore Rosato 2023 Umbria, Italy
Trebbiano Toscana and Sangiovese, one year in fibre glass. Pale pink/orange colour. Complex nose of mint, cherry and apple. The palate is textural, fine grained and well balanced, showing good restraint. 92/100

Cantina Antonioli Il Male Minore Rosso 2021 Umbria, Italy
Merlot and Cielegiolo. Vibrant and juicy with cherries, herbs and spice. Has some sour cherry and a slight bitter tang. Good energy here. 90/100

Cantina Antonioli Grabovio Rosso 2021 Umbria, Italy
13% alcohol. Merlot, Cielegiolo, Sangiovese and Barbera from limestone soils. Luicy and lively with nice acidity. Tart redcurrant and cherry fruit with a slight savoury twist. Supple and linear with a sour cherry finish. 92/100

Cantina Antonioli Agreste Elegante 2021 Umbria, Italy
13.5% alcohol. Merlot, Sangiovese, Cielegiolo, Barbera from clay soils. Juicy, bright and lively with nice tart cherry and plum fruit as well as some structure. Vivid raspberry notes, too. Nice balance with the juicy fruit to the fore. 93/100

title: La Biancara di Angiolino e Alessandro Maule: meet one of Italy’s most celebrated natural wine producers
summary: Website: https://www.angiolinomaule.com/en-home Angiolino Maule is a leading figure in the Italian natural wine scene. He and his wife Rosamaria began
content:
Website: https://www.angiolinomaule.com/en-home

Angiolino Maule is a leading figure in the Italian natural wine scene. He and his wife Rosamaria began a pizzeria in the late 1970s, and saved enough money to buy a farm, La Biancara, with some vineyards. They kept running their hospitality operation, and made their first wines, conventionally, in 1988. But they became friends with winegrowers who were trying to make wines naturally, and this appealed to them. It wasn’t an easy journey, but they persevered and Angiolino founded the VinNatur wine association in 2006. It’s one of the most forward-thinking of all the natural wine associations, and has a research element where new ways of working in the vineyard and winery are being trialled and measured.

They have 18 hectares in 13 plots around the town of Gambellara, which is in the Veneto, just northeast of Soave. Garganega is the main grape variety here, but they also have some Merlot and some Grenache (known here as Tai rosso).

Angiolino is helped out by two of his four sons who work with him in the vineyard and cellar, Alessandro and Tommaso, pictured above.

Angiolino Maule Garg’n’Go Garganega Frizzante 2024 Veneto, Italy
A sparkling wine refermented in bottle with must. Lively, bright, appley and delicious, with fresh citrus fruit and a hint of grape. Rich and expressive. 91/100

Angiolino Maule Masieri 2024 Veneto, Italy
11% alcohol. This is the entry level wine, direct pressed to stainless steel. No added sulfites. Lovely freshness here with apple and pear fruit, showing some grapey depth. Nice freshness with a bit of salinity. Good purity and massive drinkability. 93/100

Angiolino Maule Sassaia 2024 Veneto, Italy
11.5% alcohol. One day skin fermentation then fermented in 3000 litre oak. No added sulfites. Golden in colour, this is rich, smooth and textured with nice depth of ripe pear fruit and some peachy richness. Has a touch of melon, too. Harmonious and broad with nice depth. 94/100

Angiolino Taibane 2021 Maule Veneto, Italy
13.5% alcohol. This is not made every year. Harvested at the end of November, this shows nuts, honey and spice with some spicy oak. A very rich style with a savoury edge. 91/100

Angiolino Maule Masieri Rosso 2024 Veneto, Italy
50% Merlot, 50% Tai rosso (Grenache), from the limestone hills of the Colli Berichi. Lively and fresh with supple red cherry and redcurrant fruit. Juicy and grainy with nice freshness and a hint of green. Shows good acidity. 93/100

Angiolino Maule So San 2021 Veneto, Italy
16.5% alcohol. This is Tai rosso (Grenache), macerated for a month then aged two years in big barrels. Bold and brooding with a savoury, spicy edge to the sweet berry fruits. There’s some silkiness to the fruit, but also some spice and earth, and some grip. An interesting and unique wine. 93/100

Angiolino Maule Monte Sorio 2018 Veneto, Italy
Garganega, dried for 2 months, then given some skin maceration. 70 g/l sugar. Rich and bold with notes of tea, spice, earth and some honey. Real intensity here with grippy structure and a spicy frill. 92/100

title: Netzl and Christina, two generations from the same winery in Austria’s Carnuntum region
summary: Website: https://www.netzl.com/en Many Austrian wineries are family owned, and the change of generations is a way that some use to
content:
Website: https://www.netzl.com/en

Many Austrian wineries are family owned, and the change of generations is a way that some use to reinvent themselves. That’s happening at Netzl, where Christina Netzl is making a new range of wines that exists alongside the traditional wines of her father Franz (they are pictured above). Both ranges are excellent, and it’s great to see this sort of reinvention.

The Netzl vineyard, near the village of Göttlesbrun, is 30 hectares, all farmed organically, with a split of 60:40 red to white. They focus on the hillside vineyard for their single-vineyard wines. These sites are surrounded by trees, which cools things down nicely. Soils are gravelly.

I tasted these wines with Christina at Vievinum in Vienna.

Netzl Chardonnay 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
This is a regional wine, and spends 5 months in stainless steel on full lees. Lively, bright fruit here with supple lemony notes and good acidity. 89/100

Netzel Göttlesbrunn 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
This is a blend of Chardonnay and Grüner Veltliner, with the Chardonnay spending a year in amphora (750 litre Tava) on full lees. Lovely textured and depth with pure citrus and pear fruit with some white peach richness. Fine grained. 92/100

Netzl Ried Altenberg Weissburgunder 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
Made in a mix of 500 litre tonneaux and amphorae. Complex and layered with fresh fruit, rounded texture and a slight salinity. Really good. 94/100

Netzl Ried Altenberg Chardonnay 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
Made in a mix of oak and amphorae. Lovely tension here and a bit of saltiness. Pure, fine and expressive. Delicate but with tension. 93/100

Netzl Rosanna Rosé 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
Zweigelt, half direct press, half saignée. Fresh, bright, pure, linear, quite transparent, easy. 88/100

Christina Grüner Veltliner 2024 Carnuntum, Austria
The only addition is 20 mg SO2 before bottling, and this wine is bottled with fine lees. Supple and textural with nice weight and a rounded palate. Notes of white pepper support the pear and peach fruit. 92/100

Christina Rosé 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
Zweigelt, mostly saignée. Juicy with nice weight. Fruity and expressive with red cherries and pear. Stylish and textural. 91/100

Christina Orange 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
This is Chardonnay, Sauvignon Blanc and Welschriesling, skin fermented separately and spending 3 weeks on skins. No added sulfites. Lovely weight of pear and peach fruit with notes of almond and mint. Good balance with some structure. 93/100

Christina Gelber Muskateller 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
Skin fermented, five weeks on skins, aged in 500 litre vats. Floral and grapey with nice weight and good structure. Has some pear and table grape fruit. Grippy finish. 92/100

Christina Kuhlbar 2025 Carnuntum, Austria
This is Zweigelt, with a portion direct pressed, the rest a short time on skins. A lighter-style red that’s fresh and juicy with nice texture to the cherry and plum fruit, backed up with good acidity. Stylish. 93/100

Christina St Laurent 2021 Carnuntum, Austria
11% alcohol. Whole bunch, semi-carbonic. Supple and juicy with a green hint, and nice freshness and weight on the palate. Evolving nicely, and still tart and fresh. 92/100

Christina Zweigelt 2023 Carnuntum, Austria
This is short maceration then ageing in big oak. Juicy and tart with lively sour cherry and plum fruit, with some grippy structure. Nice definition: lively and crunchy. 91/100

Netzl Rubin Carnuntum 2023 Austria
Rubin Carnuntum is a regional style of Zweigelt. This is fresh with nice texture. Bright with good concentration, this is juicy and stylish. 93/100

Netzl Prellenkirchen Blaufränkisch 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
This is made from grapes they buy in from the Spitzerberg. Concentrated, chalky and mineral with nice density. Stylish showing freshness and depth, as well as some structure. 94/100

Netzl Göttlesbrun Edes Tal 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
Zweigelt, Merlot and Syrah. Fresh, supple and spicy with nice structure. Quite classic in style. Has a chalky edge to the vivid fruit, with nice density and weight. Stylish. 93/100

Netzl St Laurent 2023 Carnuntum, Austria
Destemmed, short maceration, then goes to 500 litre barrels. Lively and rounded with supple fruit. There’s a sweet core to the palate, with nice texture and balance. 93/100

Netzl Zweigelt Ried Haidacker 1er 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
Loess and gravel soils, 10% whole cluster. Very fine, fresh and expressive with nice tannic structure and a floral edge to the fruit. Some well judged oak. 93/100

Netzl Zweigelt Ried Haidacker 1er 2020 Carnuntum, Austria
Supple and bright, and linear, with nice juiciness and some structure, finishing with a sour cherry twist. Very stylish. 94/100

Netzl Ried Bärnreiser 1er Anna-Christina 2022 Carnuntum, Austria
50% Zweigelt, 30% Blaufränkisch, 20% Merlot. Bright and elegant with nice concentration, good tannins, and nice focus and grip. Linear, with real energy, and a touch of oak. Still tightwound. 93/100

title: Filippi: natural expressions of Soave, Italy
summary: Filippi are based in Soave, and the property has 20 hectares of forest as well as 14 hectares of vines.
content:
Filippi are based in Soave, and the property has 20 hectares of forest as well as 14 hectares of vines. The highest point of the vineyard is 400 m, and this is a transition point between basaltic and limestone soils. Filippo Filippi has been making wines naturally here for more than 20 years now, and they are full of interest.

Winemaking is all in stainless steel tanks with wild yeast ferments.

Filippi Vigdagòti NV IGT Verona, Italy
11.5% alcohol. One litre bottle. Garganega. Textured and mineral with supple citrus, pear and green apple fruit, with nice spicy framing. Nutritious and delicious. 91/100

Filippi Castelcerino 2024 Soave Colli Scaligeri, Italy
11% alcohol. This is from volcanic soils. Supple, finely textured and quite mineral, with notes of lemons, green apple and a hint of melon. Nice stony mineral undercurrent to the sweet, lively fruit. 93/100

Filippi Monteseroni Garganega 2023 IGT Verona, Italy
Garganega from limestone. Sometimes this is bottled as Soave but it can fail the taste test at the commission. This is fine-grained, textural and grainy with nice acidity. So fine grained with a stony, mineral edge, showing good acidity but also some depth. Lovely. 94/100

Filippi Turbiani Trebbiano di Soave IGT Verona, Italy
This is from a massal selection of 30 year old vines in the forest, on limestone soils. Fine and expressive with a lovely mineral edge and real delicacy. Fine and stony with beautiful poise and elegance. 94/100

title: Three nice rosés: Boutari, Delaunay, Foncalieu
summary: It’s rosé season, and here are three attractive, well priced examples, including a nice one from Greece. Boutari Dianthos Xinomavro
content:
It’s rosé season, and here are three attractive, well priced examples, including a nice one from Greece.

Boutari Dianthos Xinomavro Rosé 2025 PGI Imathia, Naoussa, Greece
12.5% alcohol. This is fresh and lively with some mandarin and citrus notes, as well as red cherry and cranberry. It’s fruity and delicious, but there’s a brightness and verticality to it that makes it quite gastronomic. Very stylish and well priced, too. 90/100 (£10 Tesco)

Joël Delaunay Les Cabotines Rosé 2025 Touraine, Loire, France
12.5% alcohol. Thierry Delaunay is a well respected Touraine vigneron, and this wine is made from Pineau D’Aunis and Gamay. Pale in colour, it’s stony and bright with raspberry and cranberry fruit, as well as some juiciness. This has a delicious tartness with subtle sappy hints, and lots of gastronomic potential. 91/100

Foncalieu Le Versant Grenache Rosé 2025 IGP Pays d’Oc, France
13% alcohol. Beautifully presented with a painted label design from artist Barthélémy Lanzini. This is a deliciously expressive rose with a sweet core of cherries and berries, with a fine spicy edge. There’s texture and richness here, showing deliciousness and drinkability, but also some nice spicy detail and an innate fruit sweetness. 90/100

title: Videos: Robots in the vineyard, new technology applied
summary: Viticulture is expensive, and a lot of the cost is paying people to do quite boring, repetitive work. Of course,
content:
Viticulture is expensive, and a lot of the cost is paying people to do quite boring, repetitive work. Of course, it’s great to give people jobs, but in many regions worldwide it can be a challenge finding people, especially for seasonal tasks that require a lot of people. Here I’ve collated some YouTube videos of vineyard robotics, beginning with one of my own.

This is a film I shot of the Vitibot Bakus in operation in Burgundy – it’s now quite widely used in France

This is a robot called Frasky in operation in Italy

This is a robot called Burro Grande that can be used to mow

And here are some robots in action at Pernod Ricard’s Brancott Estate in New Zealand

Here’s a trial of a robot that can actually pick grapes, being used in the UK

And here’s another grape-picking robot

Another robot, called Traxx. This, like Bakus, is suited to low canopies and close-planted vineyards with its over-row design.

Vinbot is used to estimate yield – an important job

These are Thorvald robots used to deliver UV-C to defeat powdery mildew. They need to be used in the dark.

Same technology: UV-C robots

And here’s a trial of a legged robot! Can do rocky vineyards.

title: Highlights: two from low intervention English producer Matt Gregory
summary: Website: https://mattgregorywines.co.uk/ Matt Gregory makes wines from a single 3 hectare vineyard in Leicestershire (UK), a county not normally associated
content:
Website: https://mattgregorywines.co.uk/

Matt Gregory makes wines from a single 3 hectare vineyard in Leicestershire (UK), a county not normally associated with winegrowing. The wines are naturally made, in small quantities, and they are fabulous.

Matt Gregory Wines Little Sister NV England
9% alcohol. This is a blend of two vintages.From 2023, 20% Pinot Noir, 14% Pinot Gris and from 2024, 47% Bacchus, 10% Regent, 9% Seyval Blanc. Direct pressed Bacchus from 2024 was aged in stainless steel and then blended with co-fermented Regent and Seyval after the Winter. A single barrel of Pinot Noir and Pinot Gris from the 2023 vintage was also then blended in. The only addition was a small sulfur dioxide addition before bottling in August 2025. This is pale in colour, and light and expressive on the nose with green-tinged red cherries, cranberries and elderflower. The palate is fresh and juicy with good acidity and some lovely lemon, mandarin and cherry notes, with a touch of redcurrant. It’s like a clarete in style, with more colour and structure than a rose, but it behaves quite like a white wine gastronomically, with keen acidity. The low alcohol really brings out the tart qualities of the fruit really nicely. There’s enough complexity to hold the interest, and it’s a beautifully expressive, personality-full wine. 93/100 (£27.50)

Matt Gregory Wines Two Sisters 2023 England
10% alcohol. 60/40 Pinot Noir/Pinot Gris. Destemmed and co-fermented, then pressed to stainless steel and a single used barrique from Barolo (25% of the blend). Only addition was minor sulfite add before bottling. Quite pale in colour, this has a sappy nose with green leafy notes and some cherry and redcurrant. The palate shows some structure and a hint of savoury spiciness, as well as tart red fruits, but also a bit of ripe pear richness, and some wild strawberry. There’s a lot going on here: a lighter-style red with real personality and character. 93/100 (£37.50)

title: Highlights: Champagne Gosset Cuvée Dosage Zéro
summary: Website: https://www.champagne-gosset.com/en/our-champagne-wines/cuvee-zero-dosage/ This is a new cuvée from Gosset. The wine is based on the 2013 vintage and was bottled
content:
Website: https://www.champagne-gosset.com/en/our-champagne-wines/cuvee-zero-dosage/

This is a new cuvée from Gosset. The wine is based on the 2013 vintage and was bottled in 2015, then disgorged 10 years later. The base was given more than a year on lees to add richness and complexity. This is quite beautiful.

Champagne Gosset Cuvée Dosage Zéro NV France
12% alcohol. Chardonnay 62%, Pinot Noir 38%, from a range of villages including Ambonnay, Avize, Bouzy, Cumières, Cramant, Villers-Marmery and Rilly la Montagne. Taut and expressive with lovely acidity supporting the rich, crystalline citrus fruit, together with a hint of apple and a long, tangy, slightly saline finish. This is vinous and delicious, and so fresh and gastronomic. A brilliant wine. 95/100

title: Brent Marris, Emma Marris and The Ned, tasting wines from one of Marlborough’s (NZ) emerging dynasties
summary: The Marris dynasty are an important part of Marlborough’s past, present, and surely future. John Marris was the land agent
content:
The Marris dynasty are an important part of Marlborough’s past, present, and surely future. John Marris was the land agent that Frank Yukich of (then) Montana used when he swooped in and bought some 500 hectares of lousy farmland in Marlborough, which was then not a wine region, back in 1973. It’s a remarkable story, and one I’d love to write up in more detail. The genius was that he did a lot all at once, rather than trialling a bit of land with a vineyard, then coming back for more: if farmers got hint that New Zealand’s biggest wine country thought their land was special, the price would have gone up. It turns out that John didn’t make much money despite working on a very big deal.

Brent, his son (pictured above), made his fame through working at Delgats, starting back in 1985 and launching Oyster Bay, one of Marlborough’s most successful wine brands. Then he established Wither Hills with his father, which they then sold to Lion Nathan. I first met him when he was the Wither Hills boss, along with his winemaker Ben Glover, back in 2006 (here). Fast forward 20 years and he now has an amazing property in the Waihopai Valley called Leefield Station (650 hectares of vines). From here he makes a wide range of wines, and also has a few other smaller vineyards. The Ned is the more commercial end, but here we tried the fancy end of the range, and they are excellent. The overarching brand is Marisco Vineyards.

Emma is Brent’s daughter, and now an established winemaker. She’s doing some wines under her name here, and, again, they are looking to play at a higher level. They succeed.

I tasted these wines with Brent at Wine Paris in February 2026.

Emma Marris

Emma Marris Sauvignon Blanc 2025 Marlborough, New Zealand
This is made with juice stabulation before fermentation. Lovely aromatics, with some floral character and a touch of passionfruit, leading to a palate that’s juicy, complex and nicely textured. Stylish. 93/100

Emma Marris Chardonnay 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
Hand picked and whole bunch pressed. Lovely mealy edge to the focused citrus and white peach fruit. Good balance with nice density. 93/100

Emma Marris Pinot Noir 2023 Marlborough, New Zealand
Lovely aromatics here, with a nice spicy, slightly peppery edge to the floral cherry fruit. Nice texture on the palate: silky and fine, showing good balance, appropriate structure and nice open fruit. 93/100

The Ned

The Ned 909 Sauvignon Blanc 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
This is from small parcels at Leefield Station that are a bit stonier. Hand picked before the main harvest, whole bunch pressed into a large wooden oval and amphorae, kept on yeast lees for 3 months. Very fine and expressive with lovely concentration. Stony and mineral with lovely balance and focus, with some brightness and a hint of grapefruit. 94/100

The Ned Skyscraper Sauvignon Blanc 2025 Marlborough, New Zealand
Lovely aromatics here: pure and fine with some passionfruit and just a hint of boxwood. Green notes on the palate with nice texture. Dry and finely structured with good acidity and great balance. 92/100

The Ned Skyscraper Chardonnay 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
Hand picked, warm ferment in French oak puncheons, 40% malolactic. Very stylish with lovely balance. Citrus, pear and a hint of peach. Nice mealy edge and also some structure. Subtle toastiness. 93/100

The Ned Skyscraper Pinot Noir 2024 Marlborough, New Zealand
20% whole bunch, matured in French oak puncheons, 50% new. Lovely ripe plum and cherry fruit with some berries and a bit of richness. Stylish and poised with nice texture. Very expressive. 93/100

The Ned Skyscraper Syrah 2023 Marlborough, New Zealand
Brent has planted 3 hectares of Syrah at Leefield, on a hotter spot on one of the terraces. Supple and fresh with nice brightness, showing a touch of pepper as well as sleek fruit. Expressive cool climate Syrah. 93/100

title: Brunia, an exciting cool-climate wine project from South Africa’s Walker Bay
summary: Website: https://www.bruniawines.co.za/ This is an exciting project owned by the Sander family, and it’s in a relatively unknown winegrowing region
content:
Website: https://www.bruniawines.co.za/

This is an exciting project owned by the Sander family, and it’s in a relatively unknown winegrowing region that’s called Sondagskloof. Let’s locate this geographically. It’s in the Walker Bay region, but it’s best to think of it as extension of the Cape South Coast. If you head to Hermanus on the R43, there’s a road that leads up the Hemel-en-Arde Valley, which is now a very exciting wine region with a distinctly cool climate. But you don’t take that: you keep going through the town and stay on the R43 to a town called Stanford. Then you keep going, and you find Sondagskloof about half way between Hermanus and Elim, some 17 km from the cold sea.

There’s a bit of altitude here, but not too much, and the climate is ideal for cooler-climate varieties. The farm is called Cold Mountain, and it’s quite big, but there are a modest 17 hectares of vines, from which Wade Sander (pictured above) makes some very smart wines. He trained at Elsenberg and worked a few harvests around and about, and then did six years at Mullineux before heading up this family-owned operation, alongside his brother Brett who oversees the rest of the farm. I tasted these wines with him at ProWein in March 2026.

Farming here, across the 400 hectare property, is organic and biodynamic.

Brunia Sauvignon Blanc 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
25 year old vines, all barrel fermented, with the only addition some SO2 at bottling. Textural with lovely purity. There’s a mineral core to the juicy berry fruits with some citrus brightness. Really intriguing, showing lovely weight. 94/100

Brunia White 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
50% Sauvignon, 50% Semillon, raised in barrel. Blended after 6 months and then returned to barrel for 4 more months. Fine spicy detail here: textured and lively with an appealing stony mineral edge to the ripe pear and citrus fruit. Very textural with nice concentration. 94/100

Brunia Extended Barrel Ageing Sauvignon Blanc 2019 Sondagskloof, South Africa
Lovely depth here with apples, spice and even a hint of honey. Nice depth and focus with some richness, countered by a good acid line. This is really good. 94/100

Brunia Pinot Noir 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
This is from the youngest block on the farm: 115 clone planted on sandstone 16 years ago. 10% whole cluster. Supple and elegant. Refined with some mid-palate weight, fine spicy notes and a touch of iodine. Detailed, mineral and very fine. 95/100

Brunia Syrah 2023 Sondagskloof, South Africa
40% whole bunch, then 12 months in older oak. This is lively and balanced with nice purity and some white pepper. Some fine spicy detail here. Sleek with fresh red cherry and raspberry fruit, showing real finesse. 94/100

title: Cantina Antonioli are making lovely natural wines in the foothills of the Apennine mountain range in Italy’s Umbria
summary: Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/cantinaantonioli/?hl=en Giulia and Giorgio Antonioli came to wine as a second career: she was an archaeologist and he was
content:
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/cantinaantonioli/?hl=en

Giulia and Giorgio Antonioli came to wine as a second career: she was an archaeologist and he was an art historian. But in 2018 they changed direction and rented some old vineyards on the slopes of the Apennine Mountains in Umbria (just to the south than Tuscany in the middle of the country) and renovating them.

Now Cantino Antonioli works with a selection of 60-120 year old vine plots on two different soil types: limestone and clay. The two main white varieties are Trebbiano Toscana and Malvasia.

Viticulture is enlightened, and winemaking is natural. This is a fabulous project. I tasted these wines at VinNatur in Paris, November 2025.

Cantina Antonioli Antimes Branco 2022 Umbria, Italy
This is direct pressed to cement tank, where it ferments and then ages for two years. Full yellow colour. Nice expressive pear fruit with lovely balance, showing supple citrus fruit and orange peel, with a hint of mint. Midweight, fresh and expressive, this is delicious. 92/100

Cantina Antonioli Ambratile Branco 2022 Umbria, Italy
12% alcohol. Two years in cement. Lively and bright with a stony edge to the fruit, as well as a bit of structure. Notes of apple, mandarin, orange peel and mint with an appealing wet clay/stone edge. Distinctive. 93/100

Cantina Antonioli Il Male Minore Rosato 2023 Umbria, Italy
Trebbiano Toscana and Sangiovese, one year in fibre glass. Pale pink/orange colour. Complex nose of mint, cherry and apple. The palate is textural, fine grained and well balanced, showing good restraint. 92/100

Cantina Antonioli Il Male Minore Rosso 2021 Umbria, Italy
Merlot and Cielegiolo. Vibrant and juicy with cherries, herbs and spice. Has some sour cherry and a slight bitter tang. Good energy here. 90/100

Cantina Antonioli Grabovio Rosso 2021 Umbria, Italy
13% alcohol. Merlot, Cielegiolo, Sangiovese and Barbera from limestone soils. Luicy and lively with nice acidity. Tart redcurrant and cherry fruit with a slight savoury twist. Supple and linear with a sour cherry finish. 92/100

Cantina Antonioli Agreste Elegante 2021 Umbria, Italy
13.5% alcohol. Merlot, Sangiovese, Cielegiolo, Barbera from clay soils. Juicy, bright and lively with nice tart cherry and plum fruit as well as some structure. Vivid raspberry notes, too. Nice balance with the juicy fruit to the fore. 93/100

title: La Biancara di Angiolino e Alessandro Maule: meet one of Italy’s most celebrated natural wine producers
summary: Website: https://www.angiolinomaule.com/en-home Angiolino Maule is a leading figure in the Italian natural wine scene. He and his wife Rosamaria began
content:
Website: https://www.angiolinomaule.com/en-home

Angiolino Maule is a leading figure in the Italian natural wine scene. He and his wife Rosamaria began a pizzeria in the late 1970s, and saved enough money to buy a farm, La Biancara, with some vineyards. They kept running their hospitality operation, and made their first wines, conventionally, in 1988. But they became friends with winegrowers who were trying to make wines naturally, and this appealed to them. It wasn’t an easy journey, but they persevered and Angiolino founded the VinNatur wine association in 2006. It’s one of the most forward-thinking of all the natural wine associations, and has a research element where new ways of working in the vineyard and winery are being trialled and measured.

They have 18 hectares in 13 plots around the town of Gambellara, which is in the Veneto, just northeast of Soave. Garganega is the main grape variety here, but they also have some Merlot and some Grenache (known here as Tai rosso).

Angiolino is helped out by two of his four sons who work with him in the vineyard and cellar, Alessandro and Tommaso, pictured above.

Angiolino Maule Garg’n’Go Garganega Frizzante 2024 Veneto, Italy
A sparkling wine refermented in bottle with must. Lively, bright, appley and delicious, with fresh citrus fruit and a hint of grape. Rich and expressive. 91/100

Angiolino Maule Masieri 2024 Veneto, Italy
11% alcohol. This is the entry level wine, direct pressed to stainless steel. No added sulfites. Lovely freshness here with apple and pear fruit, showing some grapey depth. Nice freshness with a bit of salinity. Good purity and massive drinkability. 93/100

Angiolino Maule Sassaia 2024 Veneto, Italy
11.5% alcohol. One day skin fermentation then fermented in 3000 litre oak. No added sulfites. Golden in colour, this is rich, smooth and textured with nice depth of ripe pear fruit and some peachy richness. Has a touch of melon, too. Harmonious and broad with nice depth. 94/100

Angiolino Taibane 2021 Maule Veneto, Italy
13.5% alcohol. This is not made every year. Harvested at the end of November, this shows nuts, honey and spice with some spicy oak. A very rich style with a savoury edge. 91/100

Angiolino Maule Masieri Rosso 2024 Veneto, Italy
50% Merlot, 50% Tai rosso (Grenache), from the limestone hills of the Colli Berichi. Lively and fresh with supple red cherry and redcurrant fruit. Juicy and grainy with nice freshness and a hint of green. Shows good acidity. 93/100

Angiolino Maule So San 2021 Veneto, Italy
16.5% alcohol. This is Tai rosso (Grenache), macerated for a month then aged two years in big barrels. Bold and brooding with a savoury, spicy edge to the sweet berry fruits. There’s some silkiness to the fruit, but also some spice and earth, and some grip. An interesting and unique wine. 93/100

Angiolino Maule Monte Sorio 2018 Veneto, Italy
Garganega, dried for 2 months, then given some skin maceration. 70 g/l sugar. Rich and bold with notes of tea, spice, earth and some honey. Real intensity here with grippy structure and a spicy frill. 92/100
